See <ref name=":0">https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RZvBadGqEbM</ref> where I link this article

[[Critical Theory]] works a lot better and has less cognitive biasses<ref name=":0" />
==References==

[[Category:John Vervaeke]]
[[sn:Conspirituality]]==[[Great November Outage]]==

See <ref name=":0">https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RZvBadGqEbM</ref> where I link this article

[[Critical Theory]] works a lot better and has less cognitive biases<ref name=":0" />
[[Category:Hydroplanes]]
[[sn:Conspirituality]][[sn:Conspirituality]]Powers and Principalities are institutions such as [[online dating]] which control our lives and lead us astray. They enhance the [[Seven Plagues]].<ref name=":0">https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RZvBadGqEbM</ref>

[[Conspirituality]] and general [[conspiracy theories]] are a product of an incorrect reaction ot the powers and principalities. Things like the conjunction bias make them happen<ref name=":0" />

== Conjunction bias reading list ==

* https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1002/acp.2995
* https://netcollegeprep.com/2019/12/03/conjunction-fallacy-a-tool-of-conspiracy-theories/
* https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/understand-overcome-bias-18-marc-bond
* https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/epdf/10.1002/acp.2995
* https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34518736/
* https://slate.com/technology/2016/12/the-conjunction-fallacy-explains-why-fake-news-is-believable.html[https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1002/acp.2995]

== References ==

[[Category:Psychology]]


==Wikipedia==

'''Conspirituality''' is a [[neologism]] [[portmanteau]] describing the overlap of [[Conspiracy theory|conspiracy theories]] with [[spirituality]], typically of [[New Age]] varieties.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|last=Love|first=Shayla|date=2020-12-16|title='Conspirituality' Explains Why the Wellness World Fell for QAnon|url=https://www.vice.com/en/article/93wq73/conspirituality-explains-why-the-wellness-world-fell-for-qanon|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201216152041/https://www.vice.com/en/article/93wq73/conspirituality-explains-why-the-wellness-world-fell-for-qanon |archive-date=2020-12-16 |access-date=2021-01-18|website=[[Vice News]]|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite web|last1=Halafoff|first1=Anna|last2=Weng|first2=Enqi|date=2020-10-13|title=COVID-19 and "(con)spirituality"|url=https://www.abc.net.au/religion/covid-conspiracies-and-conspirituality/12760976|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201222202436/https://www.abc.net.au/religion/covid-conspiracies-and-conspirituality/12760976 |archive-date=2020-12-22 |access-date=2021-01-18|website=[[ABC News (Australia)|ABC News]]|language=en}}</ref> Contemporary conspirituality became common in the 1990s.{{r|lewis2}}

==Characterization==
[[File:TZM logo.png|thumb|[[The Zeitgeist Movement]], an [[Activism|activist group]], has been called a part of the conspirituality movement.]]
The term was coined for the 2011 study "The Emergence of Conspirituality" by sociologists Charlotte Ward and [[David Voas]] published in the ''[[Journal of Contemporary Religion]]''. They characterized the movement as follows:
<blockquote>
"It offers a broad politico-spiritual philosophy based on two core convictions, the first traditional to conspiracy theory, the second rooted in the New Age: 1) a secret group covertly controls, or is trying to control, the political and social order, and 2) humanity is undergoing a 'paradigm shift' in consciousness. Proponents believe that the best strategy for dealing with the threat of a totalitarian '[[New World Order (conspiracy theory)|new world order]]' is to act in accordance with an awakened '[[new paradigm]]' worldview."<ref name=":1"/>
</blockquote>
A 2020 [[op-ed|opinion piece]] in ''[[ABC Australia]]'' said that, as with other extremist movements, the conspirituality narrative portrayed its followers as more enlightened than mainstream society and prone to persecution due to their awareness of the "real truth".{{r|fertile}} Ward and Voas considered the combination of optimistic, holistic New Age culture and pessimistic, conservative conspiracy culture to be paradoxical.<ref name="lewis2">{{cite book | editor=James Lewis | last1=Dyrendal | first1=Asbjørn| chapter=Norwegian 'Conspirituality' A Brief Sketch | title=Handbook of Nordic new religions | publisher=Brill | publication-place=Boston | year=2015 | isbn=978-90-04-29244-4 | oclc=910964138 | pages=268–290}}</ref> Conspirituality includes the "[[Occulture|dark occulture]]" of conspiracy culture.<ref name=toll>{{cite book | last1=Lewis | first1=J.R. | last2=Tollefsen | first2=I.B. | title=The Oxford Handbook of New Religious Movements: Volume II | publisher=Oxford University Press | series=Oxford Handbooks | year=2016 | isbn=978-0-19-061152-1 | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KisRDAAAQBAJ&pg=PT256 | access-date=July 17, 2021 | page=256}}</ref> The uniting philosophy of conspirituality movements is a belief that society is under the covert control by a group of elites, and that it can be emancipated from that control by a "paradigm shift in consciousness that harnesses cosmic forces".<ref name="fertile">{{cite web | website=ABC Australia| title=The pandemic has provided fertile conditions for conspiracy theories and "conspirituality" in Australia | date=October 13, 2020 | url=https://www.abc.net.au/religion/covid-conspiracies-and-conspirituality/12760976 | access-date=July 17, 2021}}</ref> The appeal of conspirituality is the narcissistic idea of being the one to unravel the true explanations for all that is wrong in the world.{{r|melt}}

Alex McKeen, writing in ''The Toronto Star'', says: {{bq|Conspiritualists share a conviction that enlightenment exists in a dimension that is separate and above politics, science and everything as banal as “three dimensional” human concerns (a common spirituality trope is reaching five-dimensional consciousness). Once you experience it — and it’s a subjective, private experience — you can’t relate anymore in “3D.”{{r|rise}}}}

Asbjørn Dyrendal counters that combining conspiracy theory with New Age spirituality is not new, and that [[Western esotericism]] is inherently suspicious. Both conspiracy culture and esotericism emphasize secrecy and the revelation of higher knowledge. He identifies [[Marta Steinsvik]], [[Alf Larsen]], [[Bertram Dybwad Brochmann]], and [[Neopaganism in Scandinavia#Norway|neo-paganism]] as early examples of the promotion of alternate spirituality and conspiracy theory.{{r|lewis2}} Jules Evans, an honorary research fellow at the Center for the History of Emotions at Queen Mary University of London, identifies an overlap between alternative spirituality and far-right populism among traditionalists.{{r|melt}}

Ward and Voas said that sometimes those with New Age beliefs are more prone to thinking like conspiracy theorists.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|last1=Ward|first1=Charlotte|last2=Voas|first2=David|date=2011-01-01|title=The Emergence of Conspirituality|journal=[[Journal of Contemporary Religion]]|volume=26|issue=1|pages=103–121|doi=10.1080/13537903.2011.539846|s2cid=143742975}}</ref> The study describes [[The Zeitgeist Movement]], an [[activist group]], as being a part of the conspirituality movement.<ref name=":1" /> Conspirituality has been linked to the [[Far-right politics|far-right]] conspiracy theory [[QAnon]] and [[Misinformation related to the COVID-19 pandemic|COVID-19 conspiracy theories]],<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" /> as well as the [[Movement for Spiritual Integration into the Absolute]] (MISA)<ref name="lewis">{{cite book | editor=James Lewis | last=Thejls | first=Sara Møldrup | chapter=MISA and Natha | title=Handbook of Nordic new religions | publisher=Brill | publication-place=Boston | year=2015 | isbn=978-90-04-29244-4 | oclc=910964138 | pages=62–76}}</ref> and the New Age religious movement [[Love Has Won]].{{r|rise}} Online yoga and wellness communities have seen members posting conspiracies about [[Covid-19]], masks, and QAnon-related child exploitation claims.<ref>{{cite news | newspaper=The New York Times | date=September 15, 2020 | last=Roose | first=Kevin | title=Yoga Teachers Take On QAnon | url=https://www.nytimes.com/2020/09/15/technology/yoga-teachers-take-on-qanon.html | access-date=July 17, 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite web | title=Why are women more hesitant to get the Covid-19 vaccine? | website=The Irish Times | url=https://www.irishtimes.com/news/health/why-are-women-more-hesitant-to-get-the-covid-19-vaccine-1.4558428?mode=amp | access-date=July 17, 2021}}</ref> [[9/11 conspiracy theories]] spread through new age communities such as "lightworkers" and "[[indigo children]]".<ref name="rise">{{cite news | newspaper=The Toronto Star | date=February 27, 2021 | title=The rise of 'conspirituality' | id={{pq|2493855989}} | last=McKeen | first=Alex }}</ref> Anthropologist of religion Dr. Adam Klin-Oron says that in Israel, "we are seeing people who used to talk about 'love' and 'light' standing shoulder to shoulder with those who believe there is a ring of pedophiles that drink the blood of babies".<ref>{{cite web | website=Haaretz | title=Iranian Accounts, Russian Tactics and Q | url=https://www.haaretz.com/israel-news/tech-news/.premium-iranian-accounts-russian-tactics-and-q-israel-has-become-a-disinfo-battlefield-1.9733592 | last=Benjakob | first=Omer | date=April 21, 2021 | access-date=July 17, 2021}}</ref>

In Norway, the online magazine {{ill|Nyhetsspeilet|no}} (''The News Mirror'') has been described{{whomst|date=November 2021}} as the "flagship of conspirituality". Its goal for "triple awakening" focuses on consciousness and spirituality, [[Ufology|extraterrestrial visitors]], and [[New World Order (conspiracy theory)|New World Order]] conspiracy theories.{{r|lewis2}} The ''Conspirituality'' podcast updates listeners on the intersection between the "wellness" industry and conspiracy theories, referring to it as "disaster spirituality".<ref>{{cite news | newspaper=The Guardian | url=https://www.theguardian.com/tv-and-radio/2021/apr/16/a-riveting-tale-of-electoral-podcasts-of-the-week | last=Phillips | first=Charlie | date=April 16, 2021 | access-date=July 17, 2021 | title=Podcasts of the week}}</ref>

==People described as members of the movement==
*[[Jake Angeli]], an American conspiracy theorist also known as the "QAnon Shaman".<ref name=melt>{{cite magazine | magazine=The Washington Post Magazine | title=QAnon's Unexpected Roots in New Age Spirituality | last=Meltzer | first=Marisa | date=March 29, 2021 | access-date=July 17, 2021 | url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/magazine/2021/03/29/qanon-new-age-spirituality/}}</ref>
*[[Pete Evans]], an Australian chef and conspiracy theorist.{{r|fertile}}
*[[David Icke]], an English conspiracy theorist{{r|lewis2}}{{r|toll}}
*[[Russell Brand]], an English comedian and activist <ref>Episode 97: Brand Awareness https://conspirituality.net/</ref>
*[[Christiane Northrup]], an [[obstetrician]] and [[gynecologist]] who promotes [[pseudoscience]]<ref>{{Cite web|last1=Bernard|first1=Nathan|last2=O'Brien|first2=Andy|date=2020-11-09|title=Dr. No|url=https://mainernews.com/dr-no/|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201109151928/https://mainernews.com/dr-no/|archive-date=2020-11-09|access-date=2021-02-17|website=[[Mainer (magazine)|Mainer]]|language=en-US}}</ref>
*[[JP Sears]], an American [[YouTuber]] and comedian<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|last=Jerry|first=Jonathan|date=2020-11-19|title=The Clown Prince of Wellness|url=https://www.mcgill.ca/oss/article/covid-19-critical-thinking-pseudoscience/clown-prince-wellness|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201121135603/https://www.mcgill.ca/oss/article/covid-19-critical-thinking-pseudoscience/clown-prince-wellness |archive-date=2020-11-21 |access-date=2021-01-18|website=[[Office for Science and Society]]|language=en}}</ref>

==See also==
*[[Pastel QAnon]]

== References ==
{{reflist}}

[[Category:Conspiracy theories]]
[[Category:New Age]]
[[Category:Far-right politics]]
[[Category:2011 neologisms]]
[[Category:Conspirituality]]
[[Category:Git synced pages]]
[[sn:Conspirituality]][[sn:Conspirituality]][[Category:Oldest pages before the refresh]]
[[Category:Pages]]
